description.text:
### Description

There are a number of text transformations that amount to repetitive tasks commonly done by authors. To give one example, if I'm quoting a brief passage that contains quotation marks…

> During this time the  meanings of "liveness" and "presence" were indistinguishable from Western discourses of modernization.

…I'll need to convert the double-quotes used in the quotation to single-quotes in my paper, like so:

> Lisa Parks argues that "during this time the  meanings of 'liveness' and 'presence' were indistinguishable from Western discourses of modernization."

This sort of thing can be done by hand or with find-replace (if find-in-region were a thing in Zettlr; see #4040). But it's also the sort of yak shaving/repetitive task that cries out for automation, particularly when using a tool like Zettlr that contains other features of advanced text editors.

### Proposed Changes

Many programming text editors contain a menu or sub-menu from which users can select text transformations that are common in programming, such as changing tabs to spaces, toggling upper and lower case characters, straightening quotes, etc. For instance, BBEdit has a killer `Text` menu:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/caa059ea-7846-462f-8763-fa0e57431161)

I'm suggesting that Zettlr add a similar menu or sub-menu populated with text transformations that are common in academic or journalistic writing. These could include:

- Double quotes to single quotes. (See above.)
- Single quotes to double quotes.
- Italics to quotes (e.g., for changing `_Cultures in Orbit_` to `"Cultures in Orbit"` to satisfy a different style guide.)
- Quotes to italics.
- Join lines (a.k.a. unfill-region, see #4039)
- Sentence case to title case.
- Title case to sentence case.

…and possibly others.

Possible locations for these menu items could include the edit menu (or a sub-menu under the edit menu), the right-click context menu when text is selected in the editor, or a top-of-screen toolbar button. Some of the most commonly used transforms might also be given their own keyboard shortcuts.

### Caveats

None.

### Do you Wish to Attempt Implementing this Yourself?

No

### Zettlr Version

Nightly

### Your Platform

- [ ] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [x] Linux

### Operating System Version

Manjaro (Rolling)

### Additional Information

_No response_

summary: Export to Word creates new page break before heading 1

description.text:
### Description

I have been using Zettlr's export to doc for a long while. Suddenly I get this strange behavior that in the Word document all level 1 headings start on a new page. I am not sure when this started to happen but it must have been sometime around the latest release. 

I exported a new custom template from Pandoc and it is the same story. It happens only to heading level 1.

### Reproducing

Use the embedded Zettlr export.docx template or your own exported custom reference from Pandoc for exporting to docx. See heading 1 starting on a new page all through the document.

I have several templates for various conferences, all based on a custom reference document I exported from Pandoc earlier. This behavior happens in all of them now.

Using the embedded Microsoft Word (docx) template from Zettlr does not show this behavior.

### Zettlr version

3.4.3

### Installation Method

Other (please specify below)

### Installation Method (Other)

From auto update in the app

### Your Platform

- [x] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [ ] Linux

### Architecture

- [x] Intel 64bit
- [ ] ARM 64bit (includes Apple Silicon)

### Operating System Version

Windows 11

### Additional Information

_No response_

## Description
Fix issue #5571 . Users can now select their favourite shortcut to open the next and previous active tab.

## Changes
GUI setting was update to allow selection of shortcut and 2 menu entries were updated to reflect the preference.
CTRL+PageUp/Down were chosen as in Visual Studio Code.

## Test
1. Open 3 documents
2. Open Preferences -> Editor, scroll to the end
3. Change the "Select next tab with" or "Select previous tab with" preferences
4. Close the Preferences window
5. Press the selected shortcut

Expected behavior: the next/previous tab is shown.

Tested on: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS

summary: Proposed UI/UX for In-Editor Notes-Bibliography Citations

description.text:
### Checklist

- [x] Zettlr does not possess this feature (if it does, but the feature doesn't work out for you, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [ ] Zettlr's current features cannot be used to approximate this behaviour (otherwise, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [ ] I have searched the documentation to make sure I did not overlook it.
- [ ] I have searched both open and closed issues to see if someone has already proposed this.

### Feature Proposal

Zettlr's in-editor citation rendering does a fantastic job of approximating author-date in-text citation styles like APA (e.g., `(West 2022)` or `West (2022)`). This is extremely useful when it comes to writing papers and articles that employ APA and similar styles because the user can include citations in their working text in a way that has high fidelity to the format in which they will eventually be exported.

However, there are prominent citation styles, like Chicago notes-bibliograpy style, that do not follow the author-date convention, but instead rely on footnotes. Writing in these styles is more cumbersome within Zettlr. I propose including in-editor citation rendering in Zettlr that approximates notes-bibliography citation styles like Chicago's, much as the current in-editor citation rendering approximates APA author-date format.

This is admittedly a bit tricky for reasons I discuss below, and this issue ticket takes a first cut at addressing the UI/UX problems involved and opening a conversation about what a solution might look like.

## How it works currently

Here's an example of how a (simple) Chicago notes-bibliography style paper currently looks in Zettlr's editor:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/da23cc81-2e9e-4c66-ac29-4b4cafc97bc9)

The first citation above is rendered from `[See @westBuyNowHow2022 for additional information.]` and the second is rendered from `[@gillespieCanAlgorithmBe2012]`. This all looks very different from the exported version:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/915eb3a1-b5c8-4a92-b0d7-3060dfb03639)

## What's cumbersome about it

There are at least three cumbersome things about this experience.

First, citation footnotes can contain long explanations — sometimes a paragraph or more — as prefixes or suffixes. And this text gets plopped right into the middle of a paragraph, which can make the resulting text hard to read. My own example — `[See @westBuyNowHow2022 for additional information.]` — is modest, but here's a real example from Erik Barnouw's _The Image Empire_:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d0ba14c5-77a6-40c4-b358-5d2c79895384)

Imagine having all the text of footnote 7 in the Barnouw snippet wedged right into the middle of a paragraph of prose in the editor. It makes it difficult to read through. (FWIW, LaTeX's `\footnote{footnote text.}` syntax has the same problem with getting plopped down in the middle of a paragraph, but it's awkward there, too. )

Second, in its current incarnation, Zettlr's citation rendering doesn't actually include the full text of the citation note. Rather, as you can see above, it truncates `[See @westBuyNowHow2022 for additional information.]` to `(See West 2022)`, leaving off the suffix portion of the text. This makes the rendering less cumbersome, but it also makes it less useable in another way — I can't actually see the full citation footnote I've written.

Third, as you can see in my original example, there are generally two kinds of footnotes going on at the same time. Footnotes that don't include any citations are inserted using the standard markdown syntax (e.g., placing a marker like `[^2]` or `[^mynote]` into the body of the text and spelling out the corresponding footnotes later in the document with the notation `[^2]: Here's my footnote.` or `[^mynote]: This is my footnote.`). In the exported document, these footnotes get zippered together with the citation footnotes inserted using the citation manager (e.g., `[See @westBuyNowHow2022 for additional information.]` and `[@gillespieCanAlgorithmBe2012]`): 

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8602b64d-3bb7-41aa-8ff1-6a714a420196)

To make matters more confusing, the footnote numbering in the export ignores the labels the user may have used. So, for example, if I had used `[^1]` or `[^3]` for the footnote with the text `Here's the text of my ordinary footnote` above, it would still be numbered `2` in the export because it appears second in the combined list. While this may all be expected behavior, the bottom line is that it's difficult for users to keep track of their footnotes while writing, since:

1. Footnotes are currently split across two separate lists.
2. One set of footnotes is wedged into the body of the document, interrupting the flow of the text, while the other appears in a separate spot, typically at the end of the file.
3. Footnote labels chosen by the user do not correspond to the footnote numbering in the exported document. Citation footnotes appear in the editor without any label, instead showing the author-date citation format `(West 2022)`, while the numbering or other labeling given to ordinary footnotes is ignored in the exported document.

## Proposed solution

To get the conversation going, here's a mockup showing one potential solution:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1fd429c7-9cd3-40b1-a77a-d520066917c2)

The upshot is that I propose a preview panel for footnotes, much like the current ones that track the document's reference list or table of contents.

In the graphic above, the first section shows the raw unrendered text entered by the user. The second shows how the text would be rendered when notes-bibliography citation rendering is selected. Here are the relevant bits:

1. Ordinary (non-citation) footnote markers are shown in the same way they currently are. E.g., `[^2]` in superscript. The text of the footnote is still entered later in the body of the document, likely near the end, in accordance with markdown's established convention.
2. Citation footnotes, while still written in-text, are collapsed to a marker when rendered, similar to the marker for an ordinary footnote. But the rendered view shows an `@` symbol instead of a `^` to indicate that the footnote is a citation footnote, not a standard markdown footnote. E.g., `[@1]` in superscript.
3. The combined list of footnotes, approximating what will be generated at export, is shown as a running list in a footnotes panel. This new footnotes panel will look and behave similar to the existing reference preview panel and may be accessible from the same sidebar. Alternatively, if a new status bar gets added, as proposed in issue #5414 , the panel could be placed in that space.
4. The rendered markers for all the footnote labels are shown as sequential numbers that correspond to what appears in the footnote preview panel. When users are creating a standard markdown footnote (as opposed to a citation footnote), they can be encouraged to use a marker number consistent with the export order via auto-completion. E.g., I begin typing `[^` and Zettlr suggests `[^6]`.
5. Clicking a footnote in the preview panel automatically focuses the footnote text wherever it may be in the editor. For example, if I click `3 Gillespie, "Limn"` in the example above, my cursor in the editor is placed on `[@gillespieCanAlgorithmBe2012]`. If I click on `4 Here's the text of my other ordinary footnote.`, my cursor jumps to the line in the document where that footnote text is shown: `[^4]: Here's the text of my other ordinary footnote.`. If the user wants to go from there to the footnote marker, they can use the existing arrow link given in the editor:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/df856c63-83be-46d5-af89-c413c735ad23)

## That's it!

Thank you, @nathanlesage and @sensologica, as always, for your thoughtful consideration of feature suggestions. I've been griping about this one long enough that I thought I should offer a constructive suggestion and, whether you implement it or not, I appreciate your thoughtfulness, commitment, and patience.

### Example Workflow

See above.

### Caveats

None.

### Do you wish to attempt implementing this yourself?

No

### Platforms

- [x] Windows
- [x] macOS
- [x] Linux

### Additional Information

_No response_

### Description

The display pane of the file manager will not show a directory. It will only display a list of recently opened files.

### Reproducing

- When navigating to File --> "New directory ..." no dialog box opens. 
- When navigating to File --> "New Workspace ..." a dialog box opens. After selecting a directory, no change is made in the file manager. 
- When navigating to the folder icon in the menu bar, a dialog box opens. After selecting a directory, no change is made in the file manager. 

After selecting "Clear FSAL cache ..." the problem persists. 

### Zettlr version

Version 3.4.3

From the Website or GitHub

summary: Quick fix: Zotero group library item cannot be open

description.text:
### Description

Recently, I noticed that when I'm right-clicking on Zotero reference in Zettlr to open it, it respond with "Could not open attachment. Is Zotero running?". So I started investigating the reason of this error and I found that if my Zotero reference is in a group library, then BetterBibTex plugin returns "<citekey> not found", and that's why Zettlr throw me an error message. I raised an issue on BetterBibTex for Zotero Github page and the answer is that when Zettlr is asking Zotero the data of the reference through BetterBibTex plugin / JSON-RPC ([here precisely](https://github.com/Zettlr/Zettlr/blob/f6bf21f9e21ed3566cc4ac44985860cbdff2440f/source/app/service-providers/commands/open-attachment.ts#L93)), if we want to search across all libraries, Zettlr need to pass `*` as `library` argument ([see `item.attachments` doc here](https://retorque.re/zotero-better-bibtex/exporting/json-rpc/index.html)).

Could someone competent and nice make this modification, please? Thanks in advance

### Reproducing

You can try to open a group library Zotero reference.

If it can helps you, you can look at the BetterBibTex plugin [issue](https://github.com/retorquere/zotero-better-bibtex/issues/3077) I raised.

### Zettlr version

3.4.2

### Installation Method

From the Website or GitHub

### Installation Method (Other)

_No response_

### Your Platform

- [ ] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [x] Linux

### Architecture

- [x] Intel 64bit

Linux Ubuntu 22.04.5

summary: Fix keymap conflicts that caused issues #5636 and #5637

description.text:
## Description
Fix issues #5636 and #5637 where conflicting keymaps would cause pressing 'Enter' to jump to a new line instead of applying autocomplete suggestions.

## Changes
The fix involved configuring the @codemirror/lang-markdown package to NOT install its Markdown keymap because this keymap took precedence over Zettlr's custom keymaps causing undesired behavior.

Tested on: macOS Sequoia 15.3.1, Apple Silicon

summary: Export to Word creates a blank first page

description.text:
### Description

Since the last update, when I export a file to Word, using a custom template, it creates a file with a blank first page.
(The blank page is not from the template, it is created by Zettlr)

The content created in Zettlr appears from page 2.

The funny thing is that the bug only appears if the .md file starts with a h1. It works fine if it starts with anything else.

### Reproducing

Create a .md file with Zettlr 3.4.2 on Windows 11 with dummy content starting with a h1.

Export to word.

### Zettlr version

3.4.2

### Installation Method

From the Website or GitHub

### Installation Method (Other)
